quotes.quote: The big theme of 2026 is that unpaid debts of the past carry interest, accruing at payday loan rates.
quotes.summary: Based on a survey of 1,101 data practitioners, Reis identifies five key trends shaping data engineering in 2026: AI becoming table stakes, a data modeling crisis demanding semantic layers, orchestration consolidation, the lakehouse-warehouse convergence, and leadership emerging as the critical bottleneck. The overarching theme is that unpaid technical and organizational debts are compounding, and the gap between disciplined and undisciplined teams is widening.
quotes.key_insight: The defining challenge of data engineering in 2026 isn't technological but organizational — teams that built disciplined foundations and secured leadership buy-in will accelerate with AI, while those carrying unaddressed debt will be buried by it.

quotes.quote: Rather than bury the results in a 30-page PDF behind a email form, I built something different: an interactive explorer where you can query the data yourself.
quotes.summary: Reis presents the results of a 1,101-response data engineering community survey, delivered as an interactive explorer rather than a traditional PDF report. Key findings reveal that while AI tool usage is nearly universal among individual practitioners, organizational AI adoption remains largely experimental, and data modeling continues to be a major pain point driven by organizational rather than technical challenges.
quotes.key_insight: AI has become ubiquitous among individual data engineers but organizational adoption is stalled, and the field's biggest obstacles remain people problems — poor leadership direction, unclear requirements, and pressure to skip modeling — not technology.

quotes.quote: The assumption that SaaS was the golden goose was based on the idea that software was hard to build and easy to rent. That dynamic has flipped. Software is now easy to build, and increasingly hard to justify renting.
quotes.summary: Reis argues that AI tools are making software trivially easy to build, transforming it from something you rent (SaaS) into something disposable you generate on demand. While this doesn't kill all software companies — those with deep data gravity, network effects, and integration ecosystems retain their moats — vendors whose value proposition is merely a workflow wrapper or single feature are facing existential threat from millions of AI-empowered builders chipping away at their offerings.
quotes.key_insight: The SaaS model depended on software being hard to build and easy to rent, but AI has inverted that equation — making software disposable and generated on demand while triggering a Jevons Paradox where easier creation drives exponentially more software into existence.

quotes.quote: Vibes are metadata, not data.
quotes.summary: This satirical post presents an elaborate, mock-serious 'technical deep dive' into building infrastructure for capturing and analyzing organizational 'vibes' at scale, complete with fake reference architectures, query languages, and maturity models. It parodies the data engineering industry's tendency to over-architect solutions, invent new categories, and wrap hype in technical jargon—all while poking fun at VC-backed infrastructure startups and the 'agentic era' buzzword cycle.
quotes.key_insight: By building a pixel-perfect parody of a data infrastructure deep dive around the absurd premise of 'vibe engineering,' the post exposes how easily the tech industry's love of complex architectures and buzzwords can be applied to justify building anything—regardless of whether it solves a real problem.

quotes.quote: I use AI to do more work… so I can make more money… to buy more tokens… to do more work.
quotes.summary: Reis argues that AI won't lead to mass unemployment but instead will trigger Parkinson's Law: as AI makes workers more efficient, the freed-up time gets filled with even more work and higher-scoped projects. He warns that companies rushing to fire workers in favor of AI will lose critical tacit knowledge and ultimately need more human effort, not less.
quotes.key_insight: AI won't replace workers so much as it will amplify Parkinson's Law — making everyone more productive just means there's more work to do, and companies that fire their workforce to cut costs will lose the tacit knowledge AI agents need to function.

quotes.quote: Most AI analytics agents fail in predictable ways. They hallucinate tables and joins, infer weird semantics from schemas, and give plausible but incorrect answers.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis stress-tested Cube's new AI analytics agent and found it performed well compared to most AI analytics tools. The key differentiator is that Cube's agent queries semantic models rather than raw schemas, operating within defined guardrails that prevent common hallucination problems.
quotes.key_insight: AI analytics agents work significantly better when they query semantic models with defined guardrails rather than improvising against raw database schemas.

quotes.quote: The market for ideas is saturated. Ideas feel scarce to the person who has them, but they're not scarce to the market.
quotes.summary: Great ideas in the data industry often fail not because they're bad, but because the market for ideas is saturated while attention is scarce. Success requires translating ideas into concrete outcomes and mastering the art of selling—clearly communicating value and reducing risk for buyers rather than talking over their heads with technical jargon.
quotes.key_insight: In a world of infinite idea supply and scarce attention, the ability to translate technical concepts into concrete, defensible outcomes is more valuable than the ideas themselves.

quotes.quote: Increase revenue, reduce cost, mitigate risk. Yep, that's it. That's the answer. It's not sexy or flashy, and won't win awards.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis reflects on how status games pervade every aspect of professional life—from leadership hierarchies to social media follower counts to expertise signaling. He argues that most status metrics (team size, titles, awards, followers) are meaningless vanity measures, and that true success for data leaders comes down to the fundamentals: increase revenue, reduce cost, mitigate risk.
quotes.key_insight: While we're all wired to play status games, authentic success in data leadership means ignoring vanity metrics and focusing on fundamental business impact—and finding a positive-sum game like education rather than zero-sum competition.

quotes.quote: In this case, AI's not an assistant, and not even a crutch. It's a wholesale lobotomy of entire roles.
quotes.summary: Reis observes that widespread AI tool adoption is creating 'brain-rotted zombies' - workers and students who have outsourced thinking to ChatGPT, losing their ability to generate original ideas or understand their own output. He warns of a growing 'competency debt bubble' as students who AI'd their way through school enter the workforce unable to actually perform, and calls for a return to fundamentals like writing memos yourself and manually debugging code.
quotes.key_insight: The rapid adoption of AI tools without maintaining foundational competencies is creating a generation of workers who can use AI but cannot think independently, building a human competency debt that will eventually come due.

quotes.key_insight: The rapid adoption of AI tools without maintaining foundational competencies is creating a generation of workers who can use AI but cannot think independently, building a human competency debt that will eventually come due.

quotes.key_insight: As AI tools proliferate, engineers who rely on pushing buttons without understanding fundamentals face a dead end, while deep knowledge of ontologies and data modeling becomes increasingly valuable.

quotes.quote: Despite the marketing blitz at re:Invent and other vendor conferences, fully autonomous LLM agents are still largely a dream. The most effective 'agents' right now are just good old-fashioned deterministic software augmented with ML.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis reflects on skipping AWS re:Invent 2025 and offers a skeptical take on the 'Year of the Agent' narrative, arguing that AI agents aren't production-ready despite marketing hype. He surveys the broader tech landscape including the Google-NVIDIA hardware competition, platform enshitification, and the resurgence of ontologies and data modeling as the industry grapples with AI's reasoning limitations.
quotes.key_insight: The 2025 'Year of the Agent' hype masks the reality that autonomous AI agents remain immature, while the industry's growing disillusionment is driving a return to foundational practices like data modeling and semantic approaches.

quotes.quote: A friend who was on the original team that created and launched ChatGPT told me they figured that maybe 10,000 people would use the app. By January 2023, over 100 million people were using ChatGPT, making it the fastest-growing app in history.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis reflects on ChatGPT's third anniversary, noting how it unexpectedly became the fastest-growing app in history and fundamentally shifted the data industry's focus from topics like Data Mesh to AI. He observes that while the technology has evolved from crude chatbots to agents and context engineering, the core innovation continues to deliver real utility despite the hype cycles.
quotes.key_insight: ChatGPT's launch marked a before-and-after moment that permanently shifted the tech industry's trajectory, and despite the inevitable hype cycles and expertise bandwagons, the underlying innovation delivers real value that won't disappear even if the bubble pops.

While current AI isn't ready for full enterprise-grade development, the exponential pace of improvement means software vendors face an existential 'moat problem' as AI increasingly replicates the specific features users actually need.
quotes.key_insight: The competitive moat for software vendors is rapidly eroding not because AI will wholesale replace their products, but because it can increasingly replicate just the specific edges of functionality that users actually need.

quotes.quote: This obsession forms what I call the Pedantic Layer, the fog of war, where we endlessly quibble over minute variations of wording and meaning, in ways that float above the actual work of using data.

quotes.key_insight: Data-driven practitioners should recognize that extrapolating their own positive AI experiences to justify market-wide valuations is a logical fallacy—the technology's utility and the bubble's sustainability are separate questions.

He contends that AI can compress weeks of work into hours for tasks like data modeling, code migrations, and stakeholder analysis, and that resistance often stems from protecting budgets and kingdoms rather than legitimate technical concerns.
quotes.key_insight: The data industry's chronic underperformance makes AI adoption not just beneficial but necessary, and those resisting it are often motivated by self-interest rather than sound technical judgment.

quotes.quote: By most accounts, people would rather die than speak in public. The fear is real.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis offers practical advice for aspiring public speakers in tech and data, emphasizing that speaking confidence comes from intentional practice rather than innate talent. He recommends starting with low-stakes environments like recording yourself, podcasts, and virtual meetups before progressing to live audiences at local meetups and eventually conference CFPs, while cautioning against over-reliance on slides that can become a crutch for inexperienced speakers.
quotes.key_insight: Public speaking excellence comes not from eliminating fear but from building confidence through intentional practice, starting in low-stakes environments and gradually progressing while staying authentic to your own voice.

quotes.quote: I don't let AI write for me. I think that cheats me and the reader. It cheats me because having an AI write for me defeats the purpose of writing. Writing is thinking.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis explains his AI-augmented writing process, emphasizing that writing encompasses research, thinking, and physical writing—with AI serving as a tool for research curation, conversation, and scaffolding, but never as a replacement for the actual craft. He argues that AI should assist but not author, because writing is fundamentally about thinking in one's own voice.
quotes.key_insight: AI should augment the writing process—research, conversation, scaffolding—but the actual writing must remain human because writing is thinking, and outsourcing it to AI cheats both writer and reader.

Drawing on wisdom from Bill Inmon, he distinguishes between 'fundamentalists' who think from first principles and 'technologists' who attach themselves to specific technologies, advocating for the former approach.
quotes.key_insight: Career longevity in data comes from mastering timeless fundamentals rather than chasing constantly changing tools and trends.

quotes.quote: If you want to feel humbled, write a book. You start off feeling like you know your topic and quickly realize you're in for a harsh lesson in humility.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis reflects on the 2.5-year journey since publishing 'Fundamentals of Data Engineering,' discussing how the book emerged from frustration with tool-focused learning and became a bestseller by teaching data engineering from first principles. He shares the intense personal challenges of writing—including working through COVID in a basement during a house remodel—and how the book transformed his career while attracting both devoted readers and inevitable critics.
quotes.key_insight: Writing a fundamentals book is harder than writing an expert-level one because distilling complexity into accessible knowledge for a broad audience requires both deep expertise and radical empathy for learners at every stage.

quotes.key_insight: The data industry's obsession with new tools over foundational knowledge is causing it to regress, and only a renewed focus on time-tested principles from industry pioneers can reverse this decline.

quotes.key_insight: Organizations must resist the pressure to ship quickly at all costs and instead invest in quality practices upfront, recognizing that the short-term slowdown pays dividends through reduced rework and technical debt.

quotes.quote: It's like the tortoise and the hare approach. So I think the modular data stack, the horizontal approach, is definitely the tortoise approach. It requires a lot of open source diplomacy, a lot of coordination across projects, getting people to agree to specialize and then work together toward productive ends.
quotes.summary: The data industry faces a fundamental choice between vertically-integrated mega platforms controlled by big clouds and a horizontally-integrated ecosystem of interoperable open technologies. While horizontal integration is slower and requires extensive coordination (the 'tortoise' approach), it offers better long-term maintainability and staying power. AI complicates this picture because model training costs currently favor plugging into major vendors' APIs.
quotes.key_insight: The data industry's future hinges on whether the slow, collaborative work of building interoperable open technologies can provide a viable alternative to vertically-integrated platforms before AI consolidates power in a handful of megacorporations.

quotes.key_insight: Just as MMA forced martial artists to become multi-disciplinary fighters, the convergence of AI, data products, and cross-functional use cases is forcing data practitioners to master multiple data modeling approaches rather than specializing in a single technique.

quotes.key_insight: Before romanticizing entrepreneurship, honestly assess whether you can handle the brutal uncertainty—and if you proceed, validate with paying customers while still employed rather than recklessly 'burning the boats.'

quotes.quote: The choice of programming language is almost always irrelevant to an organization's commercial success.
quotes.summary: Programming language debates are a waste of time because they focus on irrelevant technical minutiae rather than what actually matters: whether users are happy with what you build and whether your business succeeds. The choice of programming language is almost never a factor in commercial success, and end-users don't care about your tech stack.
quotes.key_insight: Stop debating programming languages and start focusing on whether your product actually solves problems for users - that's the only metric that matters.

quotes.key_insight: Despite recurring AI hype cycles predicting the death of coding and traditional data work, history shows these technologies tend to augment rather than replace existing practices.

quotes.quote: It's not always necessary for a data model to be flawless, as long as it facilitates decisions and actions helping move the business in the right direction.
quotes.summary: The post explores the tension between striving for perfect data models versus 'good enough' models that serve business needs. Drawing from a conversation with executive Sol Rashidi, Reis argues that while European data modelers tend toward rigorous, formal approaches, American practitioners often prioritize agility and practicality—and both can be 'good' depending on context. The key is matching your data modeling approach to business realities rather than pursuing technical perfection for its own sake.
quotes.key_insight: The best data model isn't the most technically perfect one—it's the one that's fit for purpose and enables good business decisions within the constraints of your environment.

quotes.quote: Like the dark matter that you can't see, but supposedly comprises most of the universe, dark matter workflows are things you don't see that power the business.
quotes.summary: When inheriting a failed data project, the key is to resist the urge to start with technology and instead focus first on understanding stakeholder needs, uncovering 'dark matter workflows' (shadow IT solutions built outside the data stack), and investigating the political and organizational reasons the project failed. Success requires building relationships, setting realistic expectations, and making stakeholders the heroes rather than positioning the data team as saviors.
quotes.key_insight: Failed data projects are rarely just technical failures—they're organizational and political failures that require understanding stakeholder needs and building coalitions before touching the technology.

quotes.quote: If you disagree, please travel with me in a time machine to 2011, and let's negotiate an on-prem data warehouse and ETL tooling contract with a giant tech vendor of that era.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis reflects on the rise and fall of the Modern Data Stack, from its promising beginnings with Redshift in 2012 through the ZIRP-fueled explosion of undifferentiated startups, to its current status as an obsolete term. While acknowledging the MDS ultimately succeeded by normalizing cloud-native analytics, he criticizes how the era promoted poor data practices and tool sprawl over fundamentals like data modeling.
quotes.key_insight: The Modern Data Stack succeeded so thoroughly at normalizing cloud-native analytics that the term itself became obsolete, but left behind a generation of practitioners who prioritized tooling over data fundamentals.

quotes.key_insight: The Modern Data Stack succeeded so thoroughly at normalizing cloud-native analytics that the term itself became obsolete, but left behind a generation of practitioners who prioritized tooling over data fundamentals.

quotes.quote: A data model is a structured representation that organizes and standardizes data to enable and guide human and machine behavior, inform decision-making, and facilitate actions.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis proposes a new working definition of data modeling that expands beyond human-centric views to include machines as primary consumers of data models. He argues that traditional definitions focus too narrowly on analytics use cases while ignoring the vast majority of data modeling that occurs for automated systems, and advocates for a 'Mixed Model Arts' approach that draws from multiple methodologies based on situational needs.
quotes.key_insight: Data modeling must evolve from a human-centric discipline to one that equally prioritizes machine consumers, as automated systems now process the vast majority of modeled data.

quotes.quote: It's like the people who understand data know it's difficult and want to get the basics right, and the ones pushing AI are doing it for…other reasons.
quotes.summary: Joe Reis argues that the current AI hype in corporate boardrooms follows the 'Underpants Gnomes' pattern of 'AI...?...Profit' without understanding the middle steps. He observes that executives pushing AI initiatives often don't understand data fundamentals, while those who do understand data recognize how difficult it is to get basics right. Most companies can barely do basic dashboards yet dream of AI overlords running their organizations.
quotes.key_insight: The executives most excited about AI are often the ones who least understand data, while those who understand data know that most organizations haven't mastered the basics needed to make AI initiatives successful.

quotes.quote: In Data LARPs, data teams produce very little that the business uses. Often, the data team knows this.
quotes.summary: Reis argues that many data teams are essentially 'LARPing' - going through the motions of doing data work without producing anything that reaches production or delivers business value. He identifies symptoms like endless POCs and complex architectures that look good on paper but never ship, and advises data professionals to either find quick wins to break the cycle or leave for organizations where real work gets done.
quotes.key_insight: The greatest danger for data professionals isn't technical failure but organizational theater - going through the motions of 'data work' while never shipping anything that matters.

quotes.summary: Joe Reis criticizes the flood of pundits who claimed to predict the OpenAI leadership drama, arguing that genuine uncertainty should be met with humility rather than false confidence. He reflects on the nature of forecasting, LLM limitations in data management, and the realities of startup life versus the dream of building businesses.
quotes.key_insight: Intellectual humility and admitting uncertainty is far more valuable than the false confidence of pundits who chase engagement by pretending to predict unpredictable events.

quotes.key_insight: Data management needs to abandon its perfectionist mindset and embrace AI agents as the only scalable solution for managing today's data explosion.

quotes.quote: If CEOs are this disconnected from marketing - a field that's been around for decades - how connected do you think a CEO is to 'data,' which is comparatively a brand-spanking new field?
quotes.summary: Joe Reis draws a parallel between the documented disconnect between CEOs and CMOs to argue that the gap between CEOs and Chief Data Officers is likely even worse, given that data is a much newer field. He highlights that CDOs are set up for failure due to unclear role definitions, lack of empowerment, and organizational confusion about data ownership and accountability.
quotes.key_insight: The chronic executive disconnect with marketing—a mature field—suggests that organizational dysfunction around data and AI leadership is likely far worse and requires fundamental rethinking rather than incremental fixes.

quotes.summary: Joe Reis defends the continued relevance of Kimball dimensional modeling against critics who claim columnar storage and unlimited compute have made it obsolete. He argues that dismissing data modeling in favor of raw performance leads to incomprehensible data warehouses that fail to deliver business value, ultimately resulting in users reverting to Excel and data teams getting cut.
quotes.key_insight: Raw compute power and modern tooling cannot substitute for thoughtful data modeling - prioritizing query performance over conceptual understanding leads to data warehouses that fail to deliver business value.

quotes.summary: Joe Reis argues that the data industry has largely abandoned rigorous data modeling in favor of reactive, query-driven approaches that prioritize speed over resilience. quotes.key_insight: The data industry has traded disciplined data modeling for reactive, query-driven chaos, and Reis challenges practitioners to consider whether the old ways are worth preserving or if we've simply outgrown them.

quotes.key_insight: Long-term success comes from boring, consistent daily habits rather than ambitious annual resolutions - let compound interest work in your favor.

quotes.quote: When I speak with Zhamak about her vision for Data Mesh, it comes from her dystopian fear that big companies and governments control access to data. In the end, data needs to be free.
quotes.summary: WebAssembly (WASM) could enable the decentralized data sharing vision of Data Mesh by allowing databases to run directly in browsers, bypassing centralized cloud infrastructure. This mirrors the peer-to-peer ethos of the early internet, where information flowed freely before large monopolies took control.
quotes.key_insight: WASM's ability to run databases in browsers could restore the peer-to-peer ethos of the early internet to data sharing, enabling truly decentralized data products without dependence on centralized cloud providers.

quotes.quote: Abundance has a way of distracting people into the hallucination of progress. On the other hand, constraints and scarcity have a way of sharpening the mind.
quotes.summary: The era of historically low interest rates created data teams that operated as 'performative theater' with unclear mandates and questionable value delivery. Now that money is expensive again, data teams must shift from 'growth at all costs' to proving concrete business impact through the only two levers that matter: making money and reducing costs.
quotes.key_insight: The cheap money era allowed data teams to exist as performative theater without delivering real value, but rising interest rates are forcing a return to fundamentals where only teams that measurably impact revenue or costs will survive.

quotes.quote: You know simplicity when you see it. Simplicity means designing things with the fewest moving parts, making each part easy to understand and work with.
quotes.summary: Simplicity in data engineering means designing systems with the fewest moving parts while maintaining required functionality. Data engineers often fall into cycles of building complexity then seeking simplicity, but there's no reward for unnecessary complexity.
quotes.key_insight: The best data engineering solutions are defined not by what you add, but by what you can remove while still meeting requirements.

quotes.quote: My litmus test for simplicity in data engineering - Is there anything that can be removed without sacrificing your needed form and function?

quotes.quote: As my old boss told me, 'if any number is wrong, they're all wrong.'
quotes.summary: The data industry is stuck in a Groundhog Day loop, perpetually asking the same two fundamental questions: 'Do I believe the data?' and 'How is this data adding value?' Despite decades of technological advancement and the hype around data roles, these customer-centric outcomes remain elusive because data teams focus on tools and technology rather than understanding stakeholder needs.
quotes.key_insight: Data teams must shift from technology obsession to customer obsession, because believability and value are defined entirely by stakeholders, not by the sophistication of your data stack.
